text: The surgeon begins the procedure with an enterograd salpingectomy using a 5mm ligation sealing device, efficiently sealing and cutting the fallopian tubes. Next, the round ligament is opened by cutting its medial aspect, and the dissection progresses toward the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament.

The surgeon cuts the utero-ovarian ligament and the adnexal pedicle, followed by the dissection of the posterior and anterior leaves of the left broad ligament. The dissection aims to circumscribe the left hematocorpus.

The surgeon removes the fallopian tube to improve visibility of the large left hematocorpus, measuring ten centimeters in diameter. The surgical field reveals the internal iliac artery and its bifurcation, allowing for the identification and cutting of the uterine artery to reduce perioperative bleeding.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the large hematocorpus, revealing deep dissection planes and large vessels surrounding the obstructed vagina. The superficial uterine vein is visible and controlled using a ligature sealing device, aiming for a bloodless technique to maintain clear visibility of the dissecting plane.

The surgeon continues dissection toward the left uterosacral ligament, circumscribing the posterior aspect of the large hematocorpus. The vesicovaginal dissection proceeds as the bladder is pulled downward. The remnant part of the left uterosacral ligament is controlled, with no risk to the ureter due to kidney agenesis.

The surgeon carefully dissects posteriorly to visualize the lower part of the large hematocorpus. The uterine artery is controlled at the isthmic uterine area, and the left bladder pillar is cut, located just above the hematocorpus.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the paravesical fossa and controlling the superior vesical vein and artery. The peak hematocorpus becomes progressively visible, and the vesicovaginal dissection is extended downward to circumscribe the hematocorpus.
The surgeon removes the uterine corpus, providing a clearer view of the residual cervix and the obstructed left hemivagina. The supracervical hysterectomy is performed with visible precision.

The surgeon uses Podzi forceps to tract the remnant cervix and opens it with a monopolar hook, revealing the obstructed left hemivagina. The fibrotic tissue of the hematocorpus is incised, exposing a large amount of hemosiderin clot. The clot is removed, and the hemivagina is rinsed in preparation for its removal using either a ligatrol sealing device or monopolar cutlery.

The surgeon uses a ligature device for hemostasis and improved visibility while working on the fibrotic left vagina. A finger is inserted into the right vagina, which is visible, emphasizing the importance of preserving the right functional vagina. The surgeon continues with a monopolar hook to perform marsupialization of the left vagina, leaving the remnant vagina open.

The surgeon places the clot inside an endobag and removes it through the left iliac operative trocar. The anterior aspect of the obstructed vagina is removed, followed by the left supracervical hysterectomy using a 15mm morcellator. The final surgical view reveals the right ureter, right hemiuterus, and right hemivagina, while the left hemivagina remains open in the pelvis. An ovariopexy is performed to reduce the risk of ovarian torsion.
